definitions.definition: Internal trade, also known as home trade, refers to the buying and selling of goods within the boundaries of a country. It involves the exchange of goods and services between different regions or places within a country.
External trade, also known as foreign trade, involves the exchange of goods and services between a country and other countries across its international borders.
A dealer is a trader who purchases goods from the producer or another dealer and sells them to the consumer or another dealer. Dealers act as middlemen in the distribution channel.
A commission agent is a type of dealer who purchases goods from the producer and sells them to the consumer at a higher price, earning a commission for his services.
A broker is a type of dealer who facilitates the transaction between the buyer and seller, but does not take ownership of the goods. He earns a commission for his services.
An agent is a person who acts on behalf of the principal to conduct business transactions. Agents are authorized to negotiate, conclude, and perform contracts on behalf of their principals.
A consignment agent is a type of agent who purchases goods from the producer and stores them in his godown. He is paid a commission on the sale of these goods.

A C & F (Cost and Freight) agent is a type of agent who is responsible for arranging the transportation of goods from the port of import to the destination of the buyer. He is paid a commission for his services.

Factors are short-term financiers who provide cash in advance to the merchant against the security of his goods. They purchase the goods from the merchant and sell them at a higher price to recover their investment.
Discount refers to the reduction in the price of goods or services offered by the seller to the buyer. This is usually offered to encourage the buyer to make a purchase.
Cash discount is a discount offered to the buyer when payment is made within a specified time period. This is usually offered to encourage prompt payment.
Trade discount is a discount offered to the buyer by the seller to encourage bulk purchases or to clear old stock. This is usually a percentage of the list price of the goods.
List price refers to the published price of a product as per the manufacturer or supplier. This is the maximum price at which the product can be sold.
Market price refers to the current price at which a product is selling in the market. This is the price at which the buyer and seller agree to transact.
Markup refers to the addition of a certain percentage or amount to the cost price of a product to determine the selling price. This is usually done to earn a profit.

Marginal costing is a method of costing that takes into account the additional costs incurred to produce one more unit of a product. It is used to determine the selling price and make decisions regarding the production of additional units.
Cost plus pricing is a method of pricing that involves adding a certain percentage or amount to the cost price of a product to determine the selling price. This is usually done to earn a profit.
Target pricing is a method of pricing that involves setting a target price for a product based on the desired market share and profit margin. This is usually done to achieve a competitive advantage.
Price ladder refers to a series of prices at which a product is sold to different customers based on their purchasing power and willingness to pay. This is usually done to maximize sales and revenue.

Price elasticity of demand refers to the measure of how responsive the quantity demanded of a product is to a change in its price. This is usually used to determine the effect of price changes on sales and revenue.
